pattern: Because we only ever need the lexicographically-smallest (x, y) pair by value, counting frequencies once and then scanning distinct values in sorted order (rather than every unordered pair of raw elements) finds the answer without re-deriving order from scratch.
hints: Only distinct values matter for forming a pair, and their frequencies are fixed once you've counted the whole array — count first, then reason about pairs of distinct values.

Sort the distinct values. Because you want the smallest x first, and for a fixed x the smallest y next, scanning the sorted distinct values from left to right and, for each x, scanning the ones after it for the first different frequency gives the answer directly.

Stop at the very first (x, y) found by that ordered scan — it is automatically the smallest x, and among ties the smallest y, so no further comparison across candidates is needed. If the scan finishes with nothing found, every distinct value shares the same frequency and the answer is [-1, -1].

edge_inputs.description: two values, same frequency — no valid pair
for the smallest x, the next-smallest y has the SAME frequency and must be skipped in favor of a later, still-larger-frequency-differing y
all distinct values, every one appearing exactly once — same frequency across the board despite differing values
x's frequency differs from the very next value's frequency, but a later value also differs — must pick the nearest (smallest) y
all equal values — a single distinct value, no pair possible
max length (100): one value repeated 60 times, another repeated 40 times

stress.description: random arrays of varying length and value range, seeded, exercising many frequency distributions
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, size)
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 100) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]

complexity.time: O(d^2) where d is the number of distinct values
